title: SSH Brute Force Attack
description: Repeated failed SSH authentication attempts from a single source IP indicating automated password guessing.
indicators: Multiple failed SSH logins from same IP
Login attempts targeting root or admin
High frequency auth failures within short window
detection_rule: >5 failed SSH logins from same source IP within 60 seconds
response_steps: Block the source IP at the firewall immediately
Enable Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) on all SSH access
Configure fail2ban or equivalent to auto-ban IPs after 5 failed attempts
Rotate credentials for any accounts that received failed attempts
Review successful logins from same IP range for lateral movement
Add source IP to threat intelligence blocklist

title: Port Scanning / Reconnaissance
description: Systematic probing of network ports to identify open services and potential attack vectors.
indicators: Sequential port connection attempts
SYN packets without completing handshake
Connections to unusual or high-numbered ports
detection_rule: >50 distinct port connections from same IP within 60 seconds
response_steps: Log and monitor the scanning source IP
Block the source IP at the perimeter firewall
Identify which internal services were discovered
Ensure unnecessary ports are firewalled and not publicly exposed
Check for follow-up exploitation attempts from same IP
Report to threat intelligence team for IOC correlation

title: Phishing / Credential Harvesting
description: Deceptive emails or links designed to steal user credentials or deploy malware via social engineering.
indicators: Email from spoofed domain
Link redirecting to credential capture page
Unusual login from new geolocation after email open
detection_rule: >3 failed logins from same source IP within 10 minutes correlated with successful login from same IP
response_steps: Immediately reset credentials for affected users
Block the phishing domain at DNS and email gateway
Quarantine the phishing email from all inboxes
Check for OAuth token grants or app permissions added
Enable conditional access policies requiring MFA
Notify affected users and conduct awareness training
Review mail flow rules for auto-forwarding to external domains

title: Privilege Escalation Attempt
description: An attacker or malicious process attempting to gain elevated permissions beyond those initially granted.
indicators: sudo usage by non-admin user
SUID binary execution
Unexpected addition to sudoers or admin group
detection_rule: Non-privileged user executing sudo or su commands outside business hours
response_steps: Immediately isolate the affected host from the network
Revoke elevated privileges and reset account credentials
Capture a memory dump and disk image for forensic analysis
Audit sudoers file and admin group membership
Review process tree for suspicious parent-child relationships
Check for persistence mechanisms (cron jobs, systemd services, startup scripts)
Escalate to incident response team

title: Data Exfiltration
description: Unauthorized transfer of sensitive data from the environment to an external destination.
indicators: Large outbound data transfer to unknown IP
DNS tunneling patterns
Encrypted traffic to non-business domains at unusual hours
detection_rule: Outbound transfer >100MB to single external IP within 1 hour outside business hours
response_steps: Block outbound connection to destination IP immediately
Isolate the source host from the network
Identify what data was transferred using DLP logs
Preserve network flow logs and PCAP for forensics
Notify legal and compliance teams if regulated data is involved
Assess breach notification obligations under GDPR/HIPAA/PCI-DSS
Conduct full compromise assessment of source host

title: Malware / Ransomware Execution
description: Malicious code executing on a host, potentially encrypting files or establishing persistence for further attacks.
indicators: Mass file rename or extension change
High disk I/O with encryption patterns
Known malware hash or C2 beacon traffic
detection_rule: >100 file modifications per minute or known ransomware extension (.locked, .encrypted, .crypt)
response_steps: Immediately isolate the infected host — disconnect from network
Do NOT shut down — preserve volatile memory for forensics
Identify the ransomware family via hash lookup (VirusTotal, MalwareBazaar)
Check for decryptors at nomoreransom.org before paying any ransom
Restore from last known clean backup
Identify patient zero and initial infection vector
Reset all credentials that were accessible on the infected host
Patch the exploited vulnerability before reconnecting

title: Lateral Movement via Pass-the-Hash
description: Attacker uses captured NTLM hashes to authenticate to other systems without knowing the plaintext password.
indicators: NTLM authentication from unusual source
Mimikatz or similar tool execution
Admin shares accessed from non-admin workstations
detection_rule: NTLM authentication to multiple hosts from single source within 10 minutes
response_steps: Isolate all affected hosts identified in the movement chain
Force password reset for all accounts whose hashes may have been captured
Enable Protected Users security group for privileged accounts
Disable NTLM where Kerberos is available
Deploy Windows Credential Guard on endpoints
Audit SMB access logs for additional lateral movement
Check for newly created local admin accounts on affected hosts

title: Command and Control (C2) Beacon
description: Compromised host regularly communicating with attacker-controlled infrastructure using HTTP/HTTPS.
indicators: Regular outbound HTTP/S requests at fixed intervals
JA3 fingerprint matching known C2 framework
HTTP beaconing to recently registered domain
detection_rule: Outbound HTTP/S connections to same external IP at intervals <5 minutes for >30 minutes
response_steps: Block the C2 IP and domain at firewall and DNS
Isolate the beaconing host immediately
Capture process responsible for beacon traffic
Identify initial access vector and infection timeline
Search for same C2 IOCs across all hosts in environment
Submit C2 indicators to threat intelligence feeds
Conduct full forensic investigation of beaconing host

title: Insider Threat / Abnormal Data Access
description: Legitimate user account accessing significantly more data than typical baseline, suggesting malicious insider or compromised credentials.
indicators: User accessing files outside normal role scope
Bulk download from SharePoint or S3
Access at unusual hours from normal location
detection_rule: User accesses >3x their 30-day average file count within a single session
response_steps: Preserve audit logs before any account action
Engage HR and legal before confronting the user
Revoke access tokens and enforce re-authentication
Review all data accessed in the anomalous session
Check for exfiltration via email, USB, or cloud sync
Apply DLP controls to user account pending investigation
Conduct interview with manager before account suspension

title: DNS Tunneling
description: Attacker encodes data within DNS queries/responses to exfiltrate data or maintain C2 communication bypassing traditional controls.
indicators: Unusually long DNS query strings
High volume of DNS queries to single domain
Rare or newly registered domain with many subdomains
detection_rule: DNS queries with payload >100 bytes or >1000 queries/hour to single domain
response_steps: Block the suspicious domain at DNS resolver level
Identify all internal hosts querying the suspicious domain
Capture and analyze DNS traffic for encoded payload
Isolate hosts confirmed to be tunneling
Deploy DNS security solution (e.g., Cisco Umbrella, NextDNS)
Restrict internal DNS to approved resolvers only
Review firewall rules to ensure direct DNS to external resolvers is blocked

title: Web Application SQL Injection
description: Attacker injecting malicious SQL into web application inputs to extract data or execute commands on the database.
indicators: SQL syntax in HTTP request parameters
WAF alerts for SQLi patterns
Unexpected database errors in application logs
detection_rule: WAF blocks >10 SQLi attempts from same IP within 5 minutes
response_steps: Block the source IP at WAF and firewall
Review application logs to determine if injection succeeded
If successful, identify which database tables were accessed
Rotate database credentials immediately
Apply parameterized query patches to vulnerable endpoints
Scan entire application for additional SQLi vulnerabilities
Notify data protection officer if personal data was accessed

title: Unauthorized Cloud Storage Access
description: Unauthorized access to cloud object storage (S3, Azure Blob, GCS) due to misconfiguration or compromised credentials.
indicators: Public bucket access from anonymous user
GetObject calls from unexpected geolocation
Bulk download of S3 objects via API
detection_rule: More than 50 GetObject API calls from a new IP or anonymous principal within 1 hour
response_steps: Revoke public access on the affected bucket immediately
Rotate IAM credentials associated with the access
Review CloudTrail / audit logs to enumerate accessed objects
Identify whether any sensitive data was exfiltrated
Enable S3 Block Public Access at the account level
Apply bucket policies restricting access to known VPCs or IPs
Enable server-side encryption on all buckets
Notify compliance team if regulated data was exposed

title: Account Takeover / Credential Stuffing
description: Automated use of leaked username/password pairs from breached databases to gain unauthorized access to accounts.
indicators: High login failure rate followed by success
Login from IP flagged in breach databases
Multiple accounts accessed from single IP
detection_rule: Successful login preceded by >10 failures from same IP within 5 minutes
response_steps: Force password reset on all successfully compromised accounts
Invalidate all active sessions for affected accounts
Enable MFA for all affected users
Block the source IP and ASN if abuse is widespread
Check HaveIBeenPwned or similar for credential exposure
Implement CAPTCHA or rate limiting on login endpoint
Alert users to change passwords on any reused services

title: Cryptomining / Resource Hijacking
description: Unauthorized use of compute resources to mine cryptocurrency, indicating host compromise and attacker persistence.
indicators: CPU/GPU utilization consistently >90%
Outbound connections to mining pool addresses
Known miner process name or hash
detection_rule: CPU usage >85% sustained for >10 minutes with outbound connection to known mining pool port (3333, 4444, 14444)
response_steps: Kill the mining process immediately
Isolate the host to prevent further network-based spread
Identify the initial infection vector (vulnerable service, phishing)
Remove miner binary and any associated persistence mechanisms
Check cron jobs, startup scripts, and scheduled tasks
Patch the exploited vulnerability
Review cloud billing for unexpected compute charges

title: Zero-Day Exploit Attempt
description: Exploitation attempt targeting a previously unknown or unpatched vulnerability in software or hardware.
indicators: IDS signature for known exploit pattern
Process crash followed by unexpected child process
Memory corruption patterns in application logs
detection_rule: Application process spawning shell or interpreter as unexpected child process
response_steps: Immediately isolate affected systems
Activate incident response team and escalate to CISO
Apply virtual patching via WAF or IPS rule if available
Capture memory dump before any remediation
Disable the vulnerable service if business impact allows
Monitor for vendor advisory and emergency patch
Share IOCs with threat intelligence community (ISAC)
Conduct threat hunt across all systems running same software version

title: Active Directory Kerberoasting
description: Attacker requests Kerberos service tickets for service accounts to crack offline and obtain plaintext passwords.
indicators: Bulk TGS requests for service principal names
RC4 encryption requested for AES-capable accounts
Single user requesting tickets for many SPNs
detection_rule: Single account requesting >5 service tickets (TGS-REQ) within 1 minute
response_steps: Identify the source account making excessive TGS requests
Reset passwords for all targeted service accounts immediately
Enforce AES256 encryption for all Kerberos service tickets
Implement Group Managed Service Accounts (gMSA) for service accounts
Audit all SPNs and remove unnecessary registrations
Enable audit logging for Kerberos ticket events (Event ID 4769)
Check if any service account credentials were subsequently used

title: Container Escape Attempt
description: Attempt to break out of a container sandbox to access the underlying host or other containers.
indicators: Container process accessing host filesystem paths
Privileged container executing unexpected syscalls
Docker socket mounted inside container
detection_rule: Container process accessing /proc/1, /host, or /var/run/docker.sock
response_steps: Immediately terminate the suspicious container
Inspect the host node for signs of compromise
Review container runtime logs for escape technique used
Audit all running containers for privileged mode or dangerous mounts
Enforce Pod Security Standards (restricted profile) in Kubernetes
Remove Docker socket mounts from all non-essential containers
Apply seccomp and AppArmor profiles to all production containers
Rotate all secrets that were accessible within the container

title: Supply Chain / Dependency Confusion
description: Malicious package published to public registry with same name as internal package, causing build systems to pull compromised code.
indicators: New version of internal package pulled from public registry
Package hash mismatch from expected artifact
Build pipeline executing unexpected network calls
detection_rule: CI/CD pipeline pulling package from public registry that matches internal namespace
response_steps: Immediately halt affected build pipelines
Identify all systems that installed the malicious package
Isolate and forensically analyze any deployed artifacts
Reserve the package name on public registries to prevent future attacks
Configure package manager to prefer internal registry over public
Implement package allowlisting in CI/CD
Audit all dependencies for similar naming conflicts
Rotate any secrets accessible during the compromised build

title: DDoS / Volumetric Attack
description: Overwhelming network or application infrastructure with traffic to cause denial of service for legitimate users.
indicators: Traffic volume exceeding baseline by >10x
High rate of requests from distributed IPs
Application response times >10x normal latency
detection_rule: Inbound traffic >10Gbps or HTTP request rate >10,000 req/sec sustained for >2 minutes
response_steps: Activate DDoS mitigation service (Cloudflare, AWS Shield, Akamai)
Enable rate limiting at CDN and load balancer level
Identify and null-route the largest contributing source ranges
Notify upstream ISP for BGP blackholing if volumetric
Scale out origin infrastructure if application-layer attack
Enable CAPTCHA challenges for suspicious traffic patterns
Document attack vectors for post-incident report
Review and update DDoS runbook after mitigation

title: Exposed Credentials in Source Code
description: Hardcoded API keys, passwords, or tokens discovered in source code repositories, exposing systems to unauthorized access.
indicators: Secret scanning alert from GitHub/GitLab
API key pattern in committed file
Credentials in .env file pushed to public repo
detection_rule: Secret scanning tool detects high-entropy string matching API key pattern in repository commit
response_steps: Immediately revoke and rotate the exposed credentials
Assume the credentials have been compromised — check access logs
Remove the secret from git history using git filter-repo or BFG
If public repo, treat as fully compromised regardless of exposure time
Enable secret scanning on all repositories
Implement pre-commit hooks to prevent future secret commits
Add .env and secrets files to .gitignore
Audit all services using the rotated credential for unauthorized activity
